user_goals_summary: This user writes donor-facing communications that center community members as active agents, use narrative to earn emotional and financial investment, and treat donors as decision-makers rather than recipients of gratitude. They prioritize specificity over sentimentality and insist on structural sequencing where story precedes statistics and the call to action ties directly back to the narrative.

rubric.name: Agency & Power in Language
rubric.description: Community members, local partners, and program participants are the grammatical subjects of sentences, doing active verbs — they enrolled, chose, built, pursued. The organization appears in supporting clauses, not as the hero. The subject of the sentence encodes who holds power, and the user treats this as non-negotiable.

dimensions.label: Community members and local partners are the grammatical subjects of key sentences, not the organization
Participants are described with active verbs (enrolled, chose, walked, built) rather than passive constructions or verbs of reception
The organization is positioned as enabler or supporter of community-driven action, not the primary actor
No sentences frame participants as things that programs happened to — literacy is something they pursued, not something delivered to them

rubric.name: Narrative-First Structure
rubric.description: The piece opens with one specific person's story — fully, without any statistics — before the lens widens to show scale. The reader meets a real person and sits with their choices before encountering any numbers. Numbers arrive in a separate section as evidence that this is not just one story.

dimensions.label: The piece opens with a specific individual — named, situated, making a choice — before any programmatic framing
No enrollment figures, program statistics, or scale numbers appear until after the opening narrative is complete
The individual story and the scale numbers occupy distinct sections, not the same paragraph or breath
Statistics land harder because the reader already cares about one person — the numbers feel like evidence, not a report

rubric.name: Specific, Story-Tied Call to Action
rubric.description: The ask connects directly to the narrative — a specific funding amount, a specific number of people, a specific timeframe — not a generic appeal. The reader should be able to draw a straight line from the opening story to what their next gift would do. The ask feels earned by the narrative, not appended to it.

dimensions.label: The ask includes a specific dollar amount or funding threshold
The ask names a specific number of people or families it would reach
The ask includes an honest, concrete timeframe for when the impact would be felt
The ask explicitly connects back to the person or moment from the opening narrative — the reader sees the thread
The call to action could not be copy-pasted into a different organization's newsletter without modification

rubric.name: Donor Addressed as Decision-Maker
rubric.description: The donor is spoken to directly throughout the piece — not just at the opening thank-you or closing ask. Their role is framed around their decision to invest, not their generosity. The language acknowledges that the donor's choice enabled community capacity, rather than flattering them or positioning them as saviors.

dimensions.label: The donor is addressed in second person ('you') at multiple points in the body of the piece, not only at the beginning or end
Language frames the donor's contribution around their decision or investment, not their generosity or kindness
The donor is positioned as enabling what community members already knew how to do — not as providing charity
No language positions the donor as rescuing, saving, or giving to passive recipients

rubric.name: Specificity Over Sentimentality
rubric.description: Impact is conveyed through concrete, real details — names, moments, choices, places — rather than through adjectives, emotional language, or template-style warmth. The piece should feel like it could only have been written about this program, these people, this quarter. Warmth is welcome but must be earned by specificity.

dimensions.label: Key claims are grounded in specific, verifiable details rather than vague or general language
The piece could not describe a different program without substantial rewriting — it is particular to its subject
Emotional moments arise from specific facts or narrative details, not from adjectives or declarative emotional statements
Challenges, limitations, or early-stage realities are acknowledged directly rather than glossed over

rubric.name: Connective Tissue Between Sections
rubric.description: Sections build on each other rather than standing alone. Transitions do structural work — the shift from story to scale, from scale to gap, from gap to ask each feels like a natural consequence of what came before. The closing ask should be traceable back through every preceding section.

dimensions.label: Section transitions actively bridge what came before to what comes next, rather than simply introducing a new topic
The call to action is narratively connected to the opening — the reader can trace a thread from the first paragraph to the ask
Each section raises the stakes or widens the lens in a way that depends on the section before it

coaching_notes: This user is unusually precise about sentence-level power dynamics — who is the grammatical subject of a sentence is treated as a values question, not a stylistic one. Never default to organization-as-hero framing. They prefer incremental, additive revisions over wholesale rewrites and will explicitly lock sections they're satisfied with — respect those locks. Their strongest feedback comes in the form of structural sequencing instructions (what appears before what), so always clarify section order early.

rubric.name: Narrative-First Structure
rubric.description: The piece opens with one specific person's story — fully, without any statistics — before the lens widens to show scale. The reader meets a real person and sits with their choices before encountering any numbers. Numbers arrive afterward as evidence that this is not just one story. This principle holds regardless of format — sectioned newsletters, continuous prose, or appeals.

dimensions.label: The piece opens with a specific individual — named, situated, making a choice — before any programmatic framing
No enrollment figures, program statistics, or scale numbers appear until after the opening narrative is complete
The individual story and the scale numbers are sequentially separated — the reader finishes one person's moment before the camera pulls back, whether through section breaks or paragraph transitions
Statistics land harder because the reader already cares about one person — the numbers feel like evidence, not a report

rubric.name: Specificity Over Sentimentality
rubric.description: Impact is conveyed through concrete, real details — names, moments, choices, places, times — rather than through adjectives, emotional language, or template-style warmth. Every detail should pull narrative weight; specificity serves both emotional resonance and credibility simultaneously. The piece should feel like it could only have been written about these people.

dimensions.label: Key claims are grounded in specific, verifiable details rather than vague or general language
The piece could not describe a different program without substantial rewriting — it is particular to its subject
Emotional moments arise from specific facts or narrative details, not from adjectives or declarative emotional statements
Each specific detail does narrative work — revealing character, stakes, or choices — rather than being decorative
Realities, limitations, and program status are framed accurately rather than glossed over or inflated

coaching_notes: Agency in sentence structure is the single non-negotiable — the user treats grammatical subject position as an ethical commitment, not a stylistic preference. The user values structural principles (story-first, ask-tied-to-narrative) but is flexible about format execution — headers vs. continuous prose, length, sectioning can all vary by context. When urgency is needed, the user achieves it through temporal specifics and direct address rather than emotional escalation — the rubric's existing criteria naturally produce urgency when applied to time-sensitive asks.

The transition from individual story to aggregate numbers creates a widening effect ('she's not alone') rather than a topic shift into reporting mode

rubric.name: Donor Addressed as Decision-Maker
rubric.description: The donor is spoken to directly throughout the piece — not just at the opening thank-you or closing ask. Their role is framed around their decision to invest, not their generosity. The language acknowledges that the donor's choice enabled community capacity, rather than flattering them or positioning them as saviors.

dimensions.label: The donor is addressed in second person ('you') at multiple points in the body of the piece, not only at the beginning or end
Language frames the donor's contribution around their decision or investment, not their generosity or kindness
The donor is positioned as enabling what community members already knew how to do — not as providing charity
When relevant, the donor's previous investment is connected to a specific, named current result — not just thanked in general terms
No language positions the donor as rescuing, saving, or giving to passive recipients

rubric.name: Data Integration Without Losing Voice
rubric.description: When program outcomes or harder statistics are included, they are presented in plain, direct sentences that maintain the narrative voice. Each data point does specific work — revealing trajectory, stakes, or scope — rather than being listed for comprehensiveness. The writing does not shift into report or institutional register when numbers appear.

dimensions.label: Statistics are presented in narrative sentences that maintain the piece's voice, not in tables, charts, or bullet-point report format
Key metrics include context — comparisons, baselines, or trends — so the reader understands significance, not just magnitude
Each data point serves a specific narrative function (establishing scale, showing trajectory, revealing stakes) rather than being included for comprehensiveness
The transition into and out of data-heavy passages does not shift the writing into institutional, jargon-heavy, or report-style register

coaching_notes: This user treats grammatical subject position as a non-negotiable values question — who holds the verb holds the power, and they will flag it at the sentence level every time. They operate across multiple donor communication formats (newsletters, appeals, impact reports) with a consistent set of principles they explicitly expect to transfer ('hit the same notes as the Q3 update'). Once a structural element is locked ('should not change at all'), they mean it literally — preserve locked elements and build around them. When adding data or scaling up formality, their primary concern is maintaining narrative voice and warmth; they will sacrifice comprehensiveness before sacrificing specificity or tone.

coaching_notes: Agency in sentence construction is this user's hardest non-negotiable — they will revise until participants are grammatical subjects doing active verbs, and they treat this as a power-encoding choice, not a stylistic preference. They respond well to precise structural feedback and want to be treated as a collaborator making editorial decisions, not a client receiving deliverables.

coaching_notes: Agency framing is this user's deepest non-negotiable — they will catch and reject passive constructions even when the overall draft is strong. The donor-as-decision-maker framing is equally firm: the word 'generous' or any savior-adjacent language would undermine trust. When in doubt between emotional warmth and specificity, choose specificity — this user believes concrete details generate more genuine emotion than adjectives ever could. They consistently prefer understated, forward-looking closings over effusive gratitude.

rubric.name: Agency & Power in Language
rubric.description: Community members, local partners, and program participants are the grammatical subjects of sentences, doing active verbs — they enrolled, chose, built, pursued. The organization appears in supporting clauses, not as the hero. The subject of the sentence encodes who holds power, and this user treats it as non-negotiable.

dimensions.label: Community members and local partners are the grammatical subjects of key sentences, not the organization
Participants are described with active verbs (enrolled, chose, walked, built, decided, researched, identified) rather than passive constructions or verbs of reception
The organization is positioned as enabler or supporter of community-driven action, not the primary actor
No sentences frame participants as things that programs happened to — literacy, employment, or housing is something they pursued, not something delivered to them

rubric.name: Specificity Over Sentimentality
rubric.description: Impact is conveyed through concrete, real details — names, moments, choices, places, times, logistical particulars — rather than through adjectives, emotional language, or template-style warmth. Every detail pulls narrative weight; specificity serves both emotional resonance and credibility simultaneously. The piece should feel like it could only have been written about these people.

dimensions.label: Key claims are grounded in specific, verifiable details (names, times, places, logistical particulars) that make the piece particular to its subject
Emotional moments arise from specific facts or narrative details, not from adjectives or declarative emotional statements
Each specific detail does narrative work — revealing character, stakes, or choices — rather than being decorative
Realities, limitations, and program status are framed accurately rather than glossed over or inflated
Participants are introduced through their choices, resources, or goals — not through what they lacked, lost, or suffered

rubric.name: Narrative-First Structure
rubric.description: The piece opens with one specific person's story — fully, without any statistics — before the lens widens to show scale. The reader meets a real person and sits with their choices before encountering any numbers. Numbers arrive afterward as evidence that this is not just one story. This sequencing holds regardless of format.

dimensions.label: The piece opens with a specific individual — named, situated, making a choice — before any programmatic framing or organizational context
No enrollment figures, program statistics, or scale numbers appear until after the opening narrative is complete
The individual story and the scale numbers are sequentially separated — the reader finishes one person's moment before the camera pulls back
The transition from individual story to aggregate numbers creates a widening effect ('she's not alone') rather than a topic shift into reporting mode

rubric.name: Donor Addressed as Strategic Decision-Maker
rubric.description: The donor is spoken to directly throughout the piece — not just at the opening or closing. Their role is framed around their decision to invest, not their generosity. They should feel like a strategic partner in an ongoing relationship: someone whose specific choice enabled community capacity through a tangible operational mechanism, not someone who wrote a check or performed an act of charity.

dimensions.label: The donor is addressed in second person ('you') at multiple points in the body of the piece, not only at the beginning or end
Language frames the donor's contribution around their decision or investment, not their generosity or kindness
The donor is positioned as enabling what community members already knew how to do — never as rescuing, saving, or giving to passive recipients
The connection between the donor's gift and a specific outcome is explained through a tangible operational detail (a coordinator position, a stipend, classroom hours) — not just that it helped
The donor's previous investment is connected to a specific, named current result — not just thanked in general terms

rubric.name: Specific, Story-Tied Call to Action
rubric.description: The ask connects directly to the narrative — a specific funding amount, a specific number of people, a specific timeframe — not a generic appeal. The reader should be able to draw a straight line from the opening story to what their next gift would do. The ask feels earned by the narrative and frames continued giving as a natural extension of an existing partnership, not a new transaction.

dimensions.label: The ask includes a specific dollar amount or funding threshold
The ask names a specific number of people, families, or cohort slots it would reach
The ask includes an honest, concrete timeframe for when the impact would be felt
The ask explicitly connects back to the person or moment from the opening narrative — through direct reference, structural echo, or closing callback
The ask frames the next gift as a natural next step in an ongoing partnership, not as a new or separate transaction

rubric.name: Data Integration Without Losing Voice
rubric.description: When program outcomes or harder statistics are included, they are presented in plain, direct sentences that maintain the narrative voice. Each data point does specific work — revealing trajectory, stakes, or scope — rather than being listed for comprehensiveness. The writing does not shift into report or institutional register when numbers appear.

dimensions.label: Statistics are presented in narrative sentences that maintain the piece's voice, not in tables, charts, or bullet-point report format
Key metrics include context — comparisons, baselines, or trends — so the reader understands significance, not just magnitude
Each data point serves a specific narrative function (establishing scale, showing trajectory, revealing stakes) rather than being included for comprehensiveness
The transition into and out of data-heavy passages does not shift the writing into institutional, jargon-heavy, or report-style register

writing_type: nonprofit donor communications (newsletters, appeals, impact reports, thank-you letters, recurring giving recruitment)
user_goals_summary: This user writes donor-facing communications that center the agency and choices of program participants, address donors as strategic decision-makers in an ongoing partnership, and close with specific asks tied directly to the narrative. They value specificity over sentimentality, narrative structure over reporting structure, and honest framing over emotional manipulation.
coaching_notes: Agency framing is this user's deepest non-negotiable — they will catch and reject passive constructions even when the overall draft is strong. The donor-as-decision-maker framing is equally firm: the word 'generous' or any savior-adjacent language would undermine trust. When in doubt between emotional warmth and specificity, choose specificity — this user believes concrete details generate more genuine emotion than adjectives ever could. They consistently prefer understated, forward-looking closings over effusive gratitude.
